question: Does early introduction of allergenic foods actually prevent food allergies?
topic: This corrects persistent adherence to disproven dietary recommendations, providing current evidence-

sources.name: Clinical Report: The Effects of Early Nutritional Interventions on the Development of Atopic Disease in Infants and Children — American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP)
Addendum Guidelines for the Prevention of Peanut Allergy in the United States — NIH/NIAID
Timing of allergenic food introduction to the infant diet and risk of allergic or autoimmune disease: A systematic review and meta-analysis — Cochrane Library
EAACI Food Allergy and Anaphylaxis Guidelines: Primary prevention of food allergy — European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology (EAACI)

discovery_sources.text: LEAP trial published in 2015 (over 10 years old as of 2026 episode date) - timeline is accurate
discovery_sources.origin: Unbiased Science: Food Allergies: A Tough Nut to Crack

discovery_sources.text: Early allergen introduction reduces allergy development risk - consistent with LEAP trial and subsequent research
discovery_sources.origin: Unbiased Science: Food Allergies: A Tough Nut to Crack

discovery_sources.text: 80% of children outgrow egg and milk allergies by early elementary school - plausible figure, warrants verification
discovery_sources.origin: Unbiased Science: Food Allergies: A Tough Nut to Crack

discovery_sources.text: Only 20% of children outgrow peanut allergies, barely 10% outgrow tree nut allergies - specific percentages should be verified against current literature
discovery_sources.origin: Unbiased Science: Food Allergies: A Tough Nut to Crack
